source: All-In Podcast — 2025 predictions (bestie guest Gavin Baker)
summary: The full-stack AI provider (owning compute + model) will be the ultimate AI winner; renting compute is a structural disadvantage
summary_extended: Baker argues that vertical integration of compute and frontier AI models is the decisive structural advantage in the AI race, and that the lowest-cost infrastructure provider will ultimately win. His core reasoning is that any frontier lab renting compute from a hyperscaler faces an inherent markup disadvantage — the hyperscaler charges a margin on that compute — whereas hyperscalers running their own internal AI services consume that compute at cost. He applies this principle directly to competitive positioning: OpenAI, which relies heavily on Microsoft Azure, is structurally disadvantaged relative to Google (which owns its TPUs and data centers), xAI (building its own cluster), and Amazon (which has its own silicon and cloud). The investment implication is to favor full-stack AI players — those owning both the compute layer and the model — over pure-play frontier labs that are dependent on third-party cloud infrastructure.
detail: Baker argues that the lowest-cost AI infrastructure provider wins long-term. Any frontier lab renting compute from Azure, AWS, or Google Cloud faces a structural markup disadvantage versus those providers' internal services. This implies OpenAI is structurally disadvantaged versus Google, xAI, and Amazon.
confidence_evidence: Baker states this as a clear structural principle and applies it directly to evaluate AI lab competitive positioning
quote_fragment: the ultimate AI winner will be the one with the lowest cost of infrastructure

ticker_audit.reasoning: Google is explicitly cited as a full-stack AI player owning its own compute (TPUs, data centers) and frontier models, making it a direct beneficiary of Baker's structural thesis favoring vertically integrated AI providers.
Amazon is cited as a full-stack player with its own silicon (Trainium/Inferentia) and cloud infrastructure, giving it the cost advantage Baker describes over labs renting compute.

Microsoft is directly implicated as the hyperscaler supplying compute to OpenAI via Azure, making it the structural incumbent that benefits from OpenAI's dependency — but Baker's thesis also implies OpenAI's reliance on MSFT is a disadvantage for OpenAI, making MSFT's AI positioning complex and central to the thesis.
